questions.question: Why is eviction policy choice inseparable from workload shape?
options: Because recency-heavy, popularity-heavy, and scan-heavy traces reward different policies.
Because every cache product secretly uses the same policy.
Because working-set size never matters when memory is fixed.
Because eviction only affects write latency.
questions.explanation: Policy quality depends on access patterns, not just on the policy name.

questions.question: What kind of workload often favors LRU?
options: Bursty traffic with strong short-term recency.
Purely random access with no locality.
Only immutable static assets with infinite capacity.
A workload where misses are free.
questions.explanation: LRU usually works well when recently accessed keys are likely to be accessed again soon.

questions.question: What is a classic weakness of pure LFU?
options: Yesterday's once-hot keys can linger too long if popularity shifts.
It cannot count frequencies at all.
It always performs worse than FIFO.
It makes all cache reads synchronous with the database.
questions.explanation: LFU can overvalue stale historical popularity unless aging or decay is added.

questions.question: Why can wide scans hurt an LRU cache badly?
options: They can evict many useful hot entries with one-time accesses that do not repeat soon.
They make every key immutable.
They prevent the cache from tracking hits.
They increase write durability requirements.
questions.explanation: Sequential scans pollute recency-based caches because recent does not always mean valuable.

questions.question: Which metric most directly shows whether the chosen policy is helping the business goal?
options: Hit ratio or origin offload on the highest-cost paths, not just total requests.
Only the number of cache nodes deployed.
Average code review length.
The count of named policies in documentation.
questions.explanation: A policy matters because it protects expensive origin work on important traffic, not because it sounds advanced.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of evaluating eviction with only one tiny trace?
options: You may overfit a toy example and choose a policy that fails on real skew or scan patterns.
You automatically get production-grade accuracy.
You remove the need for observability after launch.
You guarantee fairness across regions.
questions.explanation: Small traces can teach intuition, but they should not be mistaken for universal truth.

questions.question: Which answer sounds strongest in an interview?
options: LRU is industry standard, so I would use it and move on.
I would estimate working-set shape first, then choose a policy and possibly admission controls based on whether recency, frequency, or scans dominate.
I would choose FIFO because it is easiest to draw.
I would skip policy discussion because the cache vendor handles it.
questions.explanation: That answer reasons from workload shape, which is the senior-level move.

questions.question: What trade-off often comes with making the cache bigger instead of changing policy?
options: Higher memory cost and sometimes diminishing returns if the workload still contains harmful scans or trend shifts.
Perfect freshness with no additional cost.
Automatic elimination of hotspots.
No operational downside at all.
questions.explanation: More memory can help, but it does not fix every workload pathology and it costs real money.
